text: Dr. Richard Fortunato places proximal and distal stay sutures approximately eight centimeters apart on the fully mobilized colon. He opens the anti-mesenteric border of the small bowel on the specimen side, using both cautery and cutting to create a sufficient window for the stapler to navigate into the channel.

The surgeon opens the medial or mesenteric side of the colon at the midline to facilitate the stapler's navigation from the right lower quadrant 12mm port. The colon is opened on the specimen side, ensuring a sufficient window for the stapler. The surgeon holds the proximal stay suture and practices the stapler orientation with the left hand, demonstrating the wrist angles of the stapler.

The surgeon inserts the stapler, ensuring the green side is oriented towards the colon to facilitate entry into the colonic lumen. The stapler is aligned using stay sutures, and the surgeon opens it multiple times to allow the mesentery to fall away while lifting the anastomosis anteriorly. The colon is observed to fall away as the surgeon reaches underneath the stapler to pull it upward, confirming the stapler's position within the lumen by repeatedly opening and closing it.

The surgeon tests the tip of the stapler, noting the thick tissue on the small bowel side. The colon visibly falls away, confirming the stapler is in the lumen, but the bowel's redundancy on the colonic side affects the approximation.

The surgeon removes the stapler to gain better control of the bowel, using stay sutures to manipulate the colon and small bowel. The stapler is navigated into the lumens under clear visualization, ensuring proper tissue alignment and approximation. The stapler is opened to allow the mesentery to fall and the tissue to thin out. Firefly imaging is used to confirm perfusion of the anastomosis before firing the stapler. The stapler is then removed, revealing the common enterotomy, with a final check of the lumens before firing the stapler.
